graph_rag.answer: Sauti is the sage‑story‑teller who opens the Mahabharata.  He is introduced as **Ugrasrava, the son of Lomaharshana, surnamed Sauti**, a learned man “well‑versed in the Puranas.”  After the twelve‑year sacrifice of the sage Saunaka in the forest of Naimiṣa, the assembled Munis invite him to speak.  The rishis ask him where he has come from and what he wishes to relate, and Sauti replies that he will recount the sacred history of the Bharata (the Mahābhārata) as it was heard from Vyāsa and Vaisampayana【chunk_002】.  Thus, Sauti’s role in the opening of the epic is that of the **narrator who approaches the sages, is asked what they desire to hear, and then begins the recitation of the Mahābhārata**【chunk_004】.

graph_rag.answer: In the opening of the Mahābhārata the sage Vyāsa summons Ganesha, the remover of obstacles, and appoints him **the writer of the poem**.  Vyāsa asks Ganesha to “be thou the writer of the Bharata which I have formed in my imagination,” and Ganesha replies that he will write it so long as his pen never ceases.  Thus Ganesha’s function is that of the divine scribe who actually puts Vyāsa’s dictated verses onto the page, while Vyāsa continues to compose the verses.  This is reflected in the text’s statement that “Ganesa… was no sooner thought of, than he repaired to the place where Vyāsa was seated… ‘O guide of the Ganas! be thou the writer of the Bharata…’” and in the relationship “Ganesa ‑[COMPOSED]‑> Bharata”【chunk_004】.

graph_rag.answer:
The Mahābhārata opens not as a straight‑forward chronicle but as a **story‑telling frame**.  
In the first section a wandering sage named **Ugrasrava, the son of Lomaharshana – called Sauti** – arrives at the forest of Naimiṣa where the great ascetics (the Munis) have just finished Saunaka’s twelve‑year sacrifice. The assembled rishis invite him to speak, ask him where he has come from, and request that he relate the great history called *Bharata*. Sauti then bows to the deities and declares that he will now recount the sacred tale.

Thus the opening pages are framed as a **dialogue between Sauti (the narrator) and the assembled sages**:

* the **Munis** ask Sauti to tell the story,  
* Sauti replies that he will relate the Mahābhārata (the *Bharata*), invoking the gods and describing its divine origin【chunk_002】.  

So the Mahābhārata is
